companies.name: Verizon
companies.analystNotes: Reference case for the index. Years of CEI 100 scores, Pride sponsorships, and an ERG program were unwound in May 2025 when Verizon pledged to the FCC to end DEI policies the day before its $20B Frontier acquisition was approved. CEO Vestberg's 2020 'fiercely committed to diversity' statement was deleted from the web.

rationale.verdict: A longtime self-declared ally that stripped its DEI commitments in May 2025 to win FCC approval of its Frontier acquisition.
rationale.driversUp: Annual rainbow branding and Pride statements; sponsorship of Pride events; a funded LGBTQ+ employee resource group; CEI score of 100 as recently as 2024.
rationale.driversDown: In May 2025 Verizon told the FCC it would end DEI policies, scrub 'diversity, equity and inclusion' from training and public materials, and cease related sponsorships; the FCC approved its $20B Frontier deal the next day (NPR, The Intercept). CEO Hans Vestberg's 2020 statement that Verizon was 'fiercely committed to diversity and inclusion' was removed from its site (Popular Information).
rationale.decisiveFactor: The May 2025 reversal under direct regulatory pressure — support evaporated exactly when it carried a cost, proving it was conditional.

companies.analystNotes: Home of LEAGUE, one of the first LGBTQ+ ERGs in corporate America (1987), and a decade of CEI 100 scores. Quit the CEI and cut funding to LGBTQ+ groups, then formally pledged to the FCC in Dec 2025 to end DEI programs as it sought approval of its $1B+ spectrum/UScellular transactions.

rationale.verdict: Decades of pioneering LGBTQ+ workplace inclusion ended with a written pledge to the FCC to abandon DEI.
rationale.driversUp: LEAGUE at AT&T, founded 1987, is among the oldest LGBTQ+ ERGs in the US; years of CEI 100 scores; long-running Pride event sponsorships (e.g., Dallas Pride) and rainbow campaign branding.
rationale.driversDown: Stopped participating in HRC's Corporate Equality Index and ceased funding some LGBTQ+ organizations; in a Dec 1, 2025 FCC filing committed to ending DEI programs company-wide, with hiring and advancement framed as purely 'merit-based' (CNN, ESG Dive).
rationale.decisiveFactor: The December 2025 FCC letter — a formal, written renunciation of DEI made to secure deal approval.

rationale.decisiveFactor: Dropping its highest-visibility Pride commitment precisely when federal scrutiny arrived.

companies.analystNotes: Kept a deliberately low profile on LGBTQ+ issues in both directions — minimal Pride marketing, no major documented reversals. Committed to merit-based practices around its 2025 Cox merger review without a prior public LGBTQ+ identity to walk back.

companies.name: Apple
companies.analystNotes: The strongest large-cap record in the index: out CEO since 2014, annual Pride Edition products, SF Pride sponsorship, and a 97.3% shareholder vote rejecting an anti-DEI proposal in Feb 2025. Maintained its inclusion language while peers scrubbed theirs.

rationale.verdict: A sustained, structural ally that publicly held its DEI position through the 2025 backlash.
rationale.driversUp: Tim Cook has been the most prominent openly gay CEO in America since 2014; annual Apple Watch Pride Edition bands and watch faces since 2016; SF Pride sponsorship; explicit inclusion commitments still on apple.com; shareholders rejected an anti-DEI proposal 97.3% in Feb 2025 with the board's backing; CEI 100.
rationale.driversDown: Operates in countries that criminalize homosexuality without comparable advocacy there — a limit on, though not a reversal of, its commitments. No post-2025 retreat documented.
rationale.decisiveFactor: Holding the line in February 2025: Apple urged shareholders to reject the anti-DEI proposal while peers capitulated preemptively.

companies.analystNotes: Kept benefits and ERGs but ended diversity hiring targets (Feb 2025, citing federal contractor status), scrubbed DEI from its 10-K and AI pages, and deleted Pride Month from Google Calendar defaults. A partial, pressure-driven hollowing-out of a once-loud ally.

rationale.verdict: A formerly loud ally that quietly dismantled the public scaffolding of its support while keeping internal benefits.
rationale.driversUp: Two decades of SF Pride sponsorship and employee contingents; funded LGBTQ+ ERGs; early adopter of trans-inclusive healthcare (2011-era gold standard); CEI 100 streak.
rationale.driversDown: Ended diversity hiring goals in Feb 2025 citing Trump-administration contractor rules (WSJ/Axios); removed Pride Month and other observances from Google Calendar; scrubbed 'diversity' from its 10-K (8 mentions in 2023 to zero) and from its Responsible AI page; retitled its Chief Diversity Officer role.
rationale.decisiveFactor: The February 2025 decision to end diversity hiring targets under federal pressure — the pivot from advocacy to compliance.

companies.analystNotes: The most aggressive Big Tech reversal: killed DEI programs days before the 2025 inauguration and rewrote hate-speech rules to explicitly permit calling LGBTQ+ people mentally ill. GLAAD and HRC condemned the changes; trans/nonbinary Messenger themes quietly vanished.

rationale.verdict: Preemptive capitulation: Meta dismantled DEI and rewrote its rules to permit anti-LGBTQ+ abuse before Trump took office.

rationale.decisiveFactor: The January 2025 hate-speech carve-out — Meta didn't just retreat from support, it re-engineered its platform rules in ways that target LGBTQ+ users.

rationale.verdict: A steady structural ally that kept its commitments intact through the backlash years.

rationale.driversDown: Laid off an internal DEI team in July 2024 citing 'changing business needs' (Business Insider memo) — a warning sign, though public commitments and the inclusion report continued.
rationale.decisiveFactor: Continuity: while peers scrubbed pages and quit the CEI, Microsoft kept reporting and kept its programs.

rationale.decisiveFactor: The March 2025 10-K rewrite: values that defined the brand were quietly edited out under political pressure, though programs largely persist.

companies.analystNotes: From flagship Pride retailer to case study in retreat: pulled Pride merch under threats in 2023, ended DEI goals and CEI reporting in Jan 2025, then sponsored NYC Pride only as an unlisted 'silent partner.' rationale.verdict: The defining retail reversal: a decade of loud Pride allyship abandoned in stages — 2023 merch pullback, 2025 DEI termination, then anonymous-only sponsorship.
rationale.driversUp: A decade-plus of major Pride collections with queer designers, longtime Twin Cities Pride sponsorship, NYC Pride Platinum ($175K-level) sponsorship, and CEI 100 scores.
rationale.driversDown: May 2023: removed Pride items after in-store threats rather than defending them (Wikipedia/AP record). rationale.decisiveFactor: The January 2025 DEI termination — coming after the 2023 capitulation, it confirmed a pattern: Target supports Pride only when it is free.

companies.analystNotes: Doubled down while peers fled: 'This cannot change' on workforce inclusion, CEI 100 in 2025, GLAAD research partnership on LGBTQ+ ad visibility, and queer-inclusive brand campaigns (Pantene, Gillette) sustained.

rationale.verdict: The consumer-goods holder: P&G restated its inclusion commitments on the record during the retreat wave.
rationale.driversUp: Public 2024-25 reaffirmations of inclusion ('This cannot change'); CEI 100 in 2025 (HRC company page); multi-year GLAAD partnership researching LGBTQ+ inclusion in advertising; queer-inclusive campaigns across Pantene, Gillette (first trans shaving ad) and other brands; inclusive benefits.
rationale.driversDown: Brand campaigns have drawn boycott pressure and occasional quiet trims in ad placements; global footprint includes hostile markets.
rationale.decisiveFactor: Saying 'this cannot change' in writing during 2025 — explicit reaffirmation when silence was free.

companies.name: Unilever
companies.analystNotes: Brand-level support continues (Dove, Ben & Jerry's long LGBTQ+ activism) though the 2025 dispute over muzzling Ben & Jerry's political voice clouds the parent's credibility. No LGBTQ+-specific retreat documented.

rationale.verdict: Strong brand-level allyship under a parent increasingly nervous about activism generally.
rationale.driversUp: Ben & Jerry's decades of LGBTQ+ activism (marriage-equality campaigns since the 1980s-2000s); Dove inclusive campaigns; CEI 100 record; AdAge-documented year-round LGBTQ+ marketing efforts; inclusive benefits.
rationale.driversDown: 2025 public fight with Ben & Jerry's independent board over restricting its social-mission activism raises questions about how much brand-level advocacy the parent will tolerate going forward.
rationale.decisiveFactor: Brand portfolio still carries the commitment; the parent's appetite is the open question.

companies.analystNotes: Made the 'Together With Pride' GLAAD-benefiting cereal (2021, as Kellogg's) and has kept inclusive policies without joining the retreat. Modest but genuine.

rationale.verdict: A food company with a real, if episodic, Pride record and no reversal.

rationale.driversDown: Engagement is intermittent and marketing-led; limited civic or financial commitments since 2021.
rationale.decisiveFactor: No retreat plus an actual product-level donation history.

companies.name: Lockheed Martin
companies.analystNotes: First major defense contractor to publicly announce compliance with Trump's anti-DEI executive order (Jan 2025), unwinding years of CEI 100 standing and ERG visibility.

rationale.verdict: The defense sector's first public mover in dismantling DEI under the 2025 executive order.
rationale.driversUp: Years of CEI 100 scores; funded PRIDE/LGBTQ+ ERG; prior Pride event participation and inclusive benefits.
rationale.driversDown: Jan 2025: publicly announced it would abide by Trump's 'Ending Illegal Discrimination' EO while peers stayed mum (Defense Daily); removed DEI webpages as documented across the sector (Breaking Defense).
rationale.decisiveFactor: Volunteering compliance first — speed and publicity of the reversal signal priorities.

companies.analystNotes: Dismantled its global DEI department in Nov 2024 under new CEO Ortberg — before the inauguration — then joined the sector's webpage scrub. Previously CEI 100 with a Pride ERG.

rationale.verdict: A pre-inauguration dismantler: Boeing didn't wait to be pushed.

rationale.driversDown: Nov 2024: dissolved its global DEI department, folding staff into HR under CEO Kelly Ortberg amid Starbuck-amplified pressure (widely reported; TechTarget rollback tracking); 2025: DEI web presence reduced in the documented defense-sector scrub.
rationale.decisiveFactor: Eliminating the department itself months before any executive order required anything.

companies.name: Palantir Technologies
companies.analystNotes: Never engaged: no Pride programs, no CEI participation, no DEI infrastructure to remove. CEO Karp brags it was 'the first company to be completely anti-woke' (Nov 2025). Scores neutral only because the taxonomy scores actions, not attitude.

rationale.verdict: Ideologically opposed but behaviorally inert: nothing to log on either side of the ledger.
rationale.driversUp: None documented — no Pride engagement, donations, ERG visibility, or CEI participation at any point.
rationale.driversDown: No scoreable anti-LGBTQ+ corporate actions documented either; CEO Alex Karp publicly positions the company as 'the first company to be completely anti-woke' (Nov 2025 earnings call, Fortune/Fox Business), and leadership rhetoric is openly hostile to inclusion frameworks.
rationale.decisiveFactor: The taxonomy scores actions; Palantir's hostility has so far been rhetorical, leaving it at baseline with a strong caveat.
